fields.name: Autism Spectrum Disorder (ASD)
fields.definition: A group of neurodevelopmental conditions resulting in differences in communication, social understanding, and behavior. One in 100 people may have ASD, and although a person can be diagnosed at any time, symptoms generally appear in the first few years of life. DSM-5 no longer distinguishes between different types of ASD (such as Asperger's disorder or PDD-NOS); instead, a person can have ASD with or without intellectual disability. CAMH and York University emphasize that autism is not a mental health problem but a different way of communicating, understanding, and interacting with other people, and is not 'less than.' However, having ASD in environments that are not accommodating can lead to mental health difficulties.
fields.example: An adult with ASD may have deep expertise in their area of interest, prefer structured routines, and find unwritten social rules confusing, while navigating a world designed primarily for neurotypical people.

fields.name: Social Communication Differences in ASD
fields.definition: One of the two core diagnostic areas for ASD involves persistent differences in social communication and social interaction. This may include difficulty with back-and-forth conversation, reduced sharing of interests or emotions, challenges reading nonverbal communication cues (such as facial expressions, body language, and tone of voice), difficulty developing and maintaining relationships, and trouble adjusting behavior to suit different social contexts. NIMH notes that these differences exist on a spectrum and vary widely between individuals. Some people with ASD may have limited speech, while others may be highly verbal but struggle with the pragmatic (social use) aspects of language.
fields.example: A person with ASD may speak fluently about topics they are interested in but have difficulty knowing when to pause for the other person to respond, or may not pick up on sarcasm or implied meaning.

fields.name: Restricted Interests and Repetitive Behaviors in ASD
fields.definition: The second core diagnostic area for ASD involves restricted, repetitive patterns of behavior, interests, or activities. This may include repetitive motor movements or speech, insistence on sameness or inflexible adherence to routines, highly restricted and intense interests, and hyper- or hypo-reactivity to sensory input (such as apparent indifference to pain or temperature, adverse responses to specific sounds or textures, or excessive touching or smelling of objects). NIMH notes that these patterns must be present in the early developmental period, though they may not become fully apparent until social demands exceed capacity.
fields.example: A person with ASD has an intense interest in trains that has persisted since childhood, follows the same morning routine exactly every day, and becomes distressed when unexpected changes occur.

fields.body: CAMH notes that diagnosis requires a comprehensive assessment, typically conducted by a specialist such as a psychologist, developmental pediatrician, or psychiatrist with ASD expertise. To make an autism diagnosis, there must be evidence of autism during the early developmental period (ages 0 to 5). Without reliable information about early development, it is often not possible to complete the assessment. Where possible, an informant (a caregiver, parent, older sibling, or other person who knew the individual in early childhood) provides developmental history. Past assessments and school reports can also serve as documentary evidence. CAMH notes that wait times for adult autism diagnostic assessments can be approximately 2.5 years or more.
fields.name: How ASD Is Diagnosed in Adults
fields.example: An adult seeking an ASD diagnosis brings childhood school reports and their parent to the assessment appointment to provide developmental history that the person themselves may not remember.

fields.name: Causes and Brain Differences in ASD
fields.definition: ASD is a complex, lifelong neurodevelopmental condition with no single known cause. Research points to multiple contributing factors, including genetic, biological, and environmental elements. CAMH brain imaging research has revealed that differences in the spatial layout of brain networks are more pronounced among people with ASD than among those without, meaning the brains of two people with autism are more different from one another than those of two people without autism. The most variation in network location is found in the brain's attention networks. This individual variability helps explain why autism presents so differently from person to person and why one-size-fits-all treatments have shown limited success.
fields.example: Brain scans of two people with ASD may show their attention networks in different locations, helping explain why they experience different patterns of difficulty despite sharing the same diagnosis.

fields.name: Strengths-Based Understanding of ASD
fields.definition: A major CAMH/SickKids longitudinal study applied a strengths-based approach to outcome assessment, measuring proficiency and growth across five developmental areas: communication, socialization, activities of daily living, and emotional health. The study found that most children with ASD were doing well by age 10 by at least some measure. By using criteria beyond those used for diagnosis (such as ASD symptoms and cognitive ability), researchers reframed how progress in autism is conceptualized. CAMH researchers emphasize that there is no single way of 'doing well' and that 'doing well' relates to an individual's circumstances at a particular point in their life journey, not an endpoint.
fields.example: A child with ASD who has significant communication differences nonetheless shows strong growth in daily living skills and emotional regulation, representing meaningful progress that traditional assessments might miss.

fields.name: Treatment and Support for ASD
fields.definition: ASD itself is not treated as a disease to be cured, but people with ASD may benefit from various supports and interventions. CAMH's Adult Neurodevelopmental Services provide interprofessional assessment and treatment for adults with ASD and mental health concerns. The team includes psychiatrists, psychologists, behavior therapists, occupational therapists, social workers, registered nurses, and developmental service workers. Goals include supporting individuals to live in the community, addressing co-occurring mental health conditions, and providing workplace accommodations. NIMH notes that behavioral therapies, speech-language therapy, and social skills training can help people with ASD build communication and social skills.
fields.example: An adult with ASD works with an occupational therapist to develop strategies for managing sensory overload at work, while a psychiatrist addresses co-occurring anxiety.

fields.question: How common are mental health conditions in people with ASD?
fields.answer: Very common. CAMH reports that about 70% of youth with ASD have an associated emotional or behavioral problem or would meet criteria for a psychiatric condition. Depression and anxiety are the most common concerns in both youth and adults, with over 50% of adults affected. ADHD also frequently co-occurs. CAMH emphasizes that ASD in itself is not a mental health problem, but living in environments that are not accommodating can lead to mental health difficulties. Mental health presentations in autistic people can look different from those in non-autistic people, which means clinicians often miss or dismiss concerns.

fields.question: Why is Asperger's disorder no longer a separate diagnosis?
fields.answer: CAMH explains that DSM-IV included several diagnoses under pervasive developmental disorders, including Asperger's disorder, which featured autistic characteristics without intellectual disability. This categorization increased awareness of the autism spectrum and led to more people receiving an autism diagnosis. DSM-5, published in 2013, consolidated these into a single diagnosis of Autism Spectrum Disorder, with the option to specify whether intellectual disability is present. The change reflects the understanding that these conditions exist on a continuum rather than as distinct categories. People who previously received an Asperger's diagnosis now fall under ASD.

fields.question: How common is ASD?
fields.answer: CAMH reports that approximately one in 100 people may have ASD, with the average age of onset between 18 and 24 years though symptoms typically appear in the first few years of life. NIMH reports that the CDC estimates approximately 1 in 36 children in the United States have ASD, reflecting both true increases and improved identification. ASD is diagnosed more frequently in males than females, though there is growing recognition that ASD may present differently in girls and women, leading to underdiagnosis. Diagnosis rates have increased significantly over the past two decades as awareness and screening have improved.

fields.question: What barriers do adults with ASD face in accessing mental health services?
fields.answer: CAMH identifies several significant barriers. Mental health providers often do not feel competent to serve people with ASD, and few specialists are available. Wait times for adult autism diagnostic assessments at CAMH are approximately 2.5 years or more. Some individuals may not disclose their autism if they believe it will prevent them from receiving services. CAMH clinician Dr. Yona Lunsky notes that 'if you have seen one person with ASD, you have seen one person with ASD,' meaning mental health presentations are complicated and require understanding of what is typical for that individual. Clinicians are encouraged to build capacity rather than only referring to specialists.

fields.question: How should we talk about autism?
fields.answer: CAMH and York University's Mental Health Literacy Guide for Autism, developed with input from 29 autistic adults and family members across Canada, addresses appropriate language. The guide uses identity-first language ('autistic person' rather than 'person with autism') reflecting the preference of many in the autistic community. It emphasizes that autism is a different way of being, not a deficit. The guide aims to dispel the misconception that autism is a psychiatric illness, instead framing it as a neurodevelopmental difference. Appropriate language is important because how we talk about autism shapes how autistic people are understood and treated.

fields.question: What are sensory processing differences in ASD?
fields.answer: Many people with ASD experience hyper-reactivity (oversensitivity) or hypo-reactivity (undersensitivity) to sensory input. This is recognized in DSM-5 as part of the restricted/repetitive behavior criteria. Sensory differences may include adverse responses to specific sounds, textures, tastes, or visual stimuli; apparent indifference to pain or temperature; excessive touching or smelling of objects; or visual fascination with lights or movement. NIMH notes that sensory overload in environments like busy stores or offices can cause significant distress. Understanding individual sensory profiles is important for creating accommodating environments at home, school, and work.

fields.question: Is autism a mental illness that needs to be cured?
fields.answer: No. CAMH and York University explicitly state that 'autism is not a mental health problem; it is a different way of being.' It is a different way of communicating and understanding other people, but it is not less than, and it is not a psychiatric illness. However, people with ASD can and do experience mental health conditions such as anxiety and depression, often resulting from navigating environments that are not designed for them. The goal of support is not to make autistic people 'normal' but to help them live fulfilling lives, access the services they need, and thrive in environments that accommodate their differences.
